input.background: An environmental researcher wishes to measure the plant diversity in an area of 100 × 100 m in a meadow. She decides to take 100 readings using a 1 m quadrat, and considers the following three survey designs.
input.question: Dividing the sample area into ten 10 m blocks and taking one sample from a random coordinate within each of the blocks. Identify which of the designs is random, systematic or stratified-random, and state briefly any main advantages and disadvantages of this method.

output.answer:
(i) Stratified-random.

Advantage: incorporates the advantages of both of the methods below.

meta_info.book: Using Statistics to Understand the Environment

input.background: An environmental researcher wishes to measure the plant diversity in an area of 100 × 100 m in a meadow. She decides to take 100 readings using a 1 m quadrat, and considers the following three survey designs.
input.question: Taking random coordinates from the whole 100 × 100 m sample area. Identify which of the designs is random, systematic or stratified-random, and state briefly any main advantages and disadvantages of this method.

output.answer:
(ii) Random.

Advantage: reduces the opportunity for the researcher to unconsciously bias where the quadrats are placed.
Disadvantage: there may be areas of the meadow with different conditions that, by chance, are underrepresented.

meta_info.book: Using Statistics to Understand the Environment

input.background: An environmental researcher wishes to measure the plant diversity in an area of 100 × 100 m in a meadow. She decides to take 100 readings using a 1 m quadrat, and considers the following three survey designs.
input.question: Setting up ten parallel 100 m lines of quadrats (10 m apart) across the meadow with the quadrat 10 m apart on each of the lines. Identify which of the designs is random, systematic or stratified-random, and state briefly any main advantages and disadvantages of this method.

output.answer:
(iii) Systematic.

Advantage: covers all of the meadow, taking into account the possibility that conditions across the site may differ.
Disadvantage: there may be an underlying pattern of plant diversity that has a 10 m periodicity.

meta_info.book: Using Statistics to Understand the Environment

input.background: A researcher wishes to know whether there is a difference in the amount of carbon monoxide emitted from the exhausts of large and small cars.
input.question: Should he take 20 readings from a single large car and 20 from a single small car, or should he take a single reading from each of 20 large cars, and from each of 20 small cars? Briefly explain why.

output.answer: He should take a single reading from each of 20 different large cars and from each of 20 different small cars. Repeatedly sampling the same car is incorrect, since the data would be non-independent (pseudoreplication). Each of the cars may not be representative of the car population as a whole.

output.answer: Median: Genetically modified food = 3, Intensive pesticide use = 1; Lower quartile: Genetically modified food = 2, Intensive pesticide use = 1; Upper quartile: Genetically modified food = 4, Intensive pesticide use = 2.

input.background: Two sources of sediment in rivers are construction sites (which are likely to be urban in origin) and agricultural land (rural in origin). To investigate whether there was a difference in the amount of suspended sediments in urban and rural stretches of rivers, a river section in each of ten urban and rural areas was selected (each river was only used once, i.e. the data are independent). The river sections were otherwise similar in size, flow rate, altitude etc. The following sediment loads (in milligrams per litre) were obtained.
input.question: The above data have been examined and appear to be normally distributed. Use a $t$ test on the data, and record the following: Mean suspended sediment, Standard deviation, $t$ value, df, Probability.

output.answer: Mean suspended sediment: Rural = $55.2 \mathrm{mg} \mathrm{1}^{-l}$, Urban = $75.5 \mathrm{mg} \mathrm{1}^{-l}$; Standard deviation: Rural = $17.58 \mathrm{mg} \mathrm{1}^{-l}$, Urban = $21.28 \mathrm{mg} \mathrm{1}^{-l}$; $t=2.326$, $\mathrm{df}=18$, $P<0.05(=0.0319)$.

meta_info.book: Using Statistics to Understand the Environment

input.background: Two sources of sediment in rivers are construction sites (which are likely to be urban in origin) and agricultural land (rural in origin). To investigate whether there was a difference in the amount of suspended sediments in urban and rural stretches of rivers, a river section in each of ten urban and rural areas was selected (each river was only used once, i.e. the data are independent). The river sections were otherwise similar in size, flow rate, altitude etc. The following sediment loads (in milligrams per litre) were obtained.
input.question: What can you conclude about the suspended sediment levels in rural and urban rivers?

output.answer: There is significantly more suspended sediment in urban rivers than rural rivers.

meta_info.book: Using Statistics to Understand the Environment

input.background: Because persecuting badgers is an illegal activity, badger sets that are more secluded from the view of the general public may be more at risk from persecution. To test this hypothesis, badger sets were ranked as to their visibility to the nearest public right of way (footpath or road), with a score of 1 given to those which it would be impossible for people to see, and a score of 6 indicating that the set was certain to be seen. The sets were then identified as being active (i.e. with a badger social group using the set regularly) or no longer in use (where there were no recent signs of badger activity). The visibility scores for the two set types are given below.
input.question:
Use a Mann-Whitney $U$ test (because the data are ordinal) on the above data, and record the following:
\begin{tabular}{|l|l|l|}
\hline & Active & Not in use \\
\hline Median score & & \\
\hline Sample size & & \\
\hline Smaller $U$ value & & \\
\hline Probability & & \\
\hline
\end{tabular}

output.answer:
\begin{tabular}{lll}
\hline & Active & Not in use \\
\hline Median score & 3 & 2 \\
Sample size & 15 & 15 \\
Smaller $U$ value $=60.5$ & $P<0.05(=0.0310$ or 0.0256 if corrected for ties $)$ & \\
\hline
\end{tabular}

meta_info.book: Using Statistics to Understand the Environment

input.background: Because persecuting badgers is an illegal activity, badger sets that are more secluded from the view of the general public may be more at risk from persecution. To test this hypothesis, badger sets were ranked as to their visibility to the nearest public right of way (footpath or road), with a score of 1 given to those which it would be impossible for people to see, and a score of 6 indicating that the set was certain to be seen. The sets were then identified as being active (i.e. with a badger social group using the set regularly) or no longer in use (where there were no recent signs of badger activity). The visibility scores for the two set types are given below.
input.question: What do you conclude about the effect of set visibility on whether or not a set is still active?

output.answer: Active badger sets are significantly more visible than those not in use.

meta_info.book: Using Statistics to Understand the Environment

input.background: In a telephone survey, the managers of 20 small businesses were questioned on their attitude to improving the environmental nature of their products, and given a score for commitment (based on their statements and current efforts, from 0 indicating no interest, to 8 indicating a high level of interest and positive activity in that direction). Following this survey, the companies were each sent the results of research showing the attitudes that potential customers had towards environmentally friendly products of the type produced by their company. The companies were then each given a follow-up telephone survey, and their enthusiasm towards improving the environmental standard of their products was again assessed.
input.question: Use a Wilcoxon matched-pairs test on the above data (because the answers to each survey are matched for each company) and report the following: T: n: Probability:

output.answer: T=34, n=17, P<0.05 (=0.0442 or 0.0253 if corrected for ties)

meta_info.book: Using Statistics to Understand the Environment

input.background: In a telephone survey, the managers of 20 small businesses were questioned on their attitude to improving the environmental nature of their products, and given a score for commitment (based on their statements and current efforts, from 0 indicating no interest, to 8 indicating a high level of interest and positive activity in that direction). Following this survey, the companies were each sent the results of research showing the attitudes that potential customers had towards environmentally friendly products of the type produced by their company. The companies were then each given a follow-up telephone survey, and their enthusiasm towards improving the environmental standard of their products was again assessed.
input.question: What can you conclude about the attitudes of the managers of small businesses?

output.answer: Businesses were significantly more enthusiastic about improving the environmental impact of their products after receiving the results of the customer questionnaire.

meta_info.book: Using Statistics to Understand the Environment

input.background: An investigation into the possible effects on non-smokers of allowing smoking in a shared office environment.
input.question: Measured the lung capacity (in cubic metres) of the non-smokers before smoking was banned, and on the same staff a month after a ban on smoking had been enforced?

output.answer: Ideally a paired $t$ test would be employed (a Wilcoxon test could be used, but is more conservative when data are likely to be normally distributed).

meta_info.book: Using Statistics to Understand the Environment

input.background: An investigation into the possible effects on non-smokers of allowing smoking in a shared office environment.
input.question: Measured the lung capacity on a scale of 1 to 5 (where 1 was poor and 5 was excellent) of non-smoking staff in an office where smoking was allowed and similarly on staff in an adjacent office where smoking had been banned for a month?

output.answer: A Mann-Whitney $U$ test would be appropriate for this unpaired comparison of ranked scores.

meta_info.book: Using Statistics to Understand the Environment

input.background: An investigation into the possible effects on non-smokers of allowing smoking in a shared office environment.
input.question: Measured the lung capacity on a scale of 1 to 5 (where 1 was poor and 5 was excellent) of the nonsmoking office staff before smoking was banned, and on the same staff a month after a ban on smoking had been enforced?

output.answer: A Wilcoxon matched-pairs test would be suitable for these paired samples of ranked scores.

meta_info.book: Using Statistics to Understand the Environment

input.background: An investigation into the possible effects on non-smokers of allowing smoking in a shared office environment.
input.question: Measured the lung capacity (in cubic metres) of non-smoking staff in an office where smoking was allowed and similarly of non-smoking staff in an adjacent office where smoking had been banned for a month?

output.answer: Ideally a $t$ test would be employed (a Mann-Whitney $U$ test could be used, but is more conservative when data are likely to be normally distributed).

meta_info.book: Using Statistics to Understand the Environment

input.background: For several African countries, the number of physicians (per 10000 population) and the infant mortality rate (number of deaths of infants less than one year old per 1000 live births) are recorded. The data includes information for multiple countries, such as the number of physicians and the infant mortality rate.
input.question: What do you conclude about the relationship between the number of physicians and the infant mortality rate?

output.answer: There is a highly significant negative relationship between the number of physicians and the infant mortality rate: the more physicians there are, the lower the infant mortality rate. Note that we are not assuming a cause and effect relationship.

input.background: The management of a nature reserve conducted a visitor survey to gather opinions on admission charges. Two questions were posed: (1) How frequently do you visit the park? (2) What is the maximum you would be prepared to pay to enter this park? Responses were coded, and data from 20 individuals were collected.
input.question: Carry out Spearman's rank correlation on the data, and record the following: r_s, n, P.

output.answer: r_s = -0.482 (=-0.533 if corrected for ties), n = 20, P < 0.05 (=0.0203 if corrected for ties).

input.background: The management of a nature reserve conducted a visitor survey to gather opinions on admission charges. Two questions were posed: (1) How frequently do you visit the park? (2) What is the maximum you would be prepared to pay to enter this park? Responses were coded, and data from 20 individuals were collected.
input.question: What do you conclude about the relationship between the frequency of visits and the amount an individual is prepared to pay?

output.answer: There is a significant negative relationship between frequency of visit and amount the visitor is prepared to pay: the more frequent the visits, the less the visitor is prepared to pay.

input.question: To test whether fuel economy is dependent on the size of the engine, perform a regression analysis and record the following: R^2, Regression equation, F, df, and P.

output.answer: R^2=69.4%, Regression equation: Economy=22.39-5.862 Engine size (or y=22.39-5.862x), F=49.961, df=1 and 22.

\hline East & 50 & 40 & 2.5 \\
\hline South & 29 & 40 & 3.025 \\
\hline West & 45 & 40 & 0.625 \\
\hline
\end{tabular}

input.background: NO2 concentrations (in parts per billion) in the exhaust fumes of different types of vehicle (cars, minibuses and buses) were sampled and the following data were obtained.
input.question: Check that the variances of the three samples are not significantly different using an F_max test.

output.answer: The highest variance is for minibuses (30.786). The lowest variance is for cars (12.982). F_max = 2.37, k = 3, df = 7, P > 0.05; therefore the variances are not significantly different.

input.question: Calculate the mean (x̄) and standard error (SE) of NO2 concentration in each vehicle type.

output.answer: Cars: x̄ = 68.1 ppb, SE = 1.27 ppb. Minibuses: x̄ = 52.3 ppb, SE = 1.96 ppb. Buses: x̄ = 42.8 ppb, SE = 1.72 ppb.

input.background: NO2 concentrations (in parts per billion) in the exhaust fumes of different types of vehicle (cars, minibuses and buses) were sampled and the following data were obtained.
input.question: Using multiple comparison tests where appropriate, what do you conclude from your analysis of these data?

output.answer: There is a significant difference between the NO2 concentration in the different types of vehicle, with cars having the most and buses the least. Using Tukey's multiple comparisons: The MSD is smaller than the difference between any pair of means, therefore all the means are significantly different from each other (Cars > Minibuses > Buses; P < 0.05).

meta_info.chapter: Differences between more than two samples

input.background: A local council was considering introducing extensive recycling facilities. As part of a survey investigating the attitudes of the public towards the popularity of this initiative, the effect of age of the respondent was taken into account. Respondents were asked to score their enthusiasm towards recycling on a scale of 1 to 5 (where 5 was very enthusiastic) and their age was classed as: up to 25 ; from 26 to 50 ; or over 51 years.
input.question: Carry out Kruskal-Wallis one-way analysis of variance using ranks on the data.

output.answer: Rank means: Up to 25 = 18.85, 26 to 50 = 8.15, Over 51 = 19.5; df = 2; H = 10.483 (corrected: 10.984); P < 0.01 (corrected exact: 0.0041).

meta_info.chapter: Differences between more than two samples
meta_info.book: Using Statistics to Understand the Environment

input.background: A local council was considering introducing extensive recycling facilities. As part of a survey investigating the attitudes of the public towards the popularity of this initiative, the effect of age of the respondent was taken into account. Respondents were asked to score their enthusiasm towards recycling on a scale of 1 to 5 (where 5 was very enthusiastic) and their age was classed as: up to 25 ; from 26 to 50 ; or over 51 years.
input.question: Using multiple comparison tests where appropriate, what do you conclude from this analysis?

output.answer: There is a significant difference among age groups as to enthusiasm towards recycling. Nemenyi's multiple comparisons reveal that those aged up to 25 and over 51 do not differ in their enthusiasm, but both age groups are significantly more enthusiastic than those aged 26-50 (P < 0.05): (up to 25, over 51) > 26 to 50.

meta_info.chapter: Differences between more than two samples

input.background: The percentages of all forest lost per year in ten randomly selected countries in each of Central America and South East Asia are given. Half of the countries are island countries, and half are mainland countries. The arcsine-transformed data (to normalise the distributions) are given alongside the original data. The variances of the transformed data have been subjected to an $F_{\text {max }}$ test and are not significantly different.
input.question: Using multiple comparison tests where appropriate, what do you conclude from this analysis?

output.answer: Central America has a significantly higher percentage forest loss than does South East Asia and mainland countries have significantly higher loss than islands. There is no significant interaction between region and location, thus the effects of region (Central America or South East Asia) are independent of those of location (island or mainland). Note that multiple comparisons are not needed when only two categories are being combined at a time.

meta_info.chapter: Differences between more than two samples
meta_info.book: Using Statistics to Understand the Environment

input.background: The amount of nitrogen (in kilotonnes per annum) entering UK coastal waters via fresh water inputs (i.e. from rivers) is compared to that entering directly into saline waters from point sources (e.g. pipes).
input.question: Using multiple comparison tests where appropriate, what can you conclude from these results?

output.answer: River input is significantly higher than direct input (River=42; Direct=23.2), and there is a significant difference among regions. Tukey's multiple comparisons reveal that the North Sea East Coast has significantly higher nitrogen input than all the other regions (P<0.05). None of the other regions differ from each other: North Sea East Coast > (Irish Sea, Celtic Sea, Atlantic, North Sea Channel).

meta_info.chapter: Differences between more than two samples

meta_info.chapter: DIFFERENCES BETWEEN MORE THAN TWO SAMPLES
meta_info.book: Statistics for Business and Economics

input.background: An environmental pressure group contacted backbench MPs from two political parties (neither of which were in government), just before and immediately after a general election, and asked them for their views on environmental issues. The responses were analysed using content analysis. The number of positive words in each politician's statements were recorded and analysed.
input.question: Using multiple comparison tests where appropriate, what can you conclude from these results?

output.answer: There is a significant effect of timing: politicians say fewer positive words about environmental issues after the general election. There is no significant difference between the parties in the number of positive words in politicians' responses, and there is no significant interaction between timing and political party. (Multiple comparisons are not necessary, because there is no interaction and only two categories in the significant factor.)

\begin{tabular}{lllllllll}
\hline & Belize & Costa Rica & El Salvador & Guatemala & Honduras & Mexico & Nicaragua & Panama \\
\hline Mammals & 7.44 & 4.93 & 5.43 & 5.17 & 4.47 & 7.29 & 5.08 & 6.00 \\
Birds & 0.20 & 0.63 & 0.69 & 1.20 & 0.74 & 12.8 & 0.66 & 0.65 \\
Reptiles/ Amphibians & 6.02 & 2.44 & 5.38 & 3.30 & 4.13 & 3.90 & 4.07 & 3.27 \\
\hline
\end{tabular}

\begin{tabular}{llll}
\hline & Rank mean & \multicolumn{3}{l}{ Friedman's test } \\
\hline Mammals & & $\mathrm{df}:$ & $\mathrm{Fr}:$ & $\mathrm{P}:$ \\
Birds & & & \\
Reptiles/ amphibians & & & \\
\hline
\end{tabular}
